title: Autoencoders (AE)
learning_outcomes: Formulate the encoder-decoder architecture of autoencoders and define the role of the latent space bottleneck.
Contrast undercomplete and overcomplete autoencoders, explaining the representation constraints of each.
Formulate the reconstruction loss functions and prove the mathematical equivalence between linear autoencoders and PCA.

content_sections.title: Autoencoder Foundations & Latent Bottlenecks
content_sections.content:
An **Autoencoder** is an unsupervised neural network designed to learn a compressed representation (encoding) of an input dataset, and then reconstruct the original input from this representation.

### 1. Architectural Components
The network is split into two symmetrical components connected by a latent bottleneck layer:
*   **Encoder ($f_{\theta}$):** Projects the high-dimensional input vector $\\mathbf{x} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{D}$ to a low-dimensional latent space vector $\\mathbf{z} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{d}$:

$$\\mathbf{z} = f_{\\theta}(\\mathbf{x}) = \\sigma(\\mathbf{W}_e \\mathbf{x} + \\mathbf{b}_e)$$

*   **Decoder ($g_{\\phi}$):** Takes the latent code $\\mathbf{z}$ and projects it back to reconstruct the original input, outputting $\\hat{\\mathbf{x}} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{D}$:

$$\\hat{\\mathbf{x}} = g_{\\phi}(\\mathbf{z}) = \\sigma(\\mathbf{W}_d \\mathbf{z} + \\mathbf{b}_d)$$

### 2. The Bottleneck constraint
The central requirement is that the latent dimension $d$ is constrained. This bottleneck forces the network to discard noise and redundant features, learning only the most important semantic factors of the data manifold.

callouts.title: Copy Mapping in Identity Networks
callouts.content: Without a bottleneck or proper regularization, an autoencoder with high hidden dimensions will simply learn the identity function (copying input values directly to the output: $\\hat{\\mathbf{x}} = \\mathbf{x}$). While such a network yields a perfect reconstruction score (zero loss), it fails to learn any useful feature representations.

content_sections.title: Undercomplete vs. Overcomplete Autoencoders
content_sections.content:
The dimensional relationship between input space $D$ and latent space $d$ determines the model's structural constraints.

### 1. Undercomplete Autoencoders
An autoencoder is **undercomplete** if the latent dimension is strictly smaller than the input dimension:

$$d < D$$

*   **Mechanism:** Because the representation is compressed, the network cannot simply copy the input. It is forced to learn a low-dimensional coordinate mapping that represents the data manifold. 
*   If the encoder and decoder are linear functions, the network learns the span of the principal components of the dataset.

### 2. Overcomplete Autoencoders
An autoencoder is **overcomplete** if the latent dimension is larger than the input dimension:

$$d > D$$

*   **Mechanism:** If unregularized, an overcomplete autoencoder will learn the identity mapping and fail. However, if we restrict the capacity (e.g. by adding a sparsity constraint on the latent activations or training the network to reconstruct inputs from corrupted noise), overcomplete autoencoders can extract highly robust, high-dimensional feature spaces.

callouts.title: Denoising Autoencoders (DAE)
callouts.content: A Denoising Autoencoder is trained to reconstruct the original clean input $\\mathbf{x}$ from a corrupted version $\\tilde{\\mathbf{x}} = \\mathbf{x} + \\boldsymbol{\\epsilon}$. This forces the network to learn the geometric structure of the data manifold, mapping noisy inputs back onto the stable manifold surface.

content_sections.title: Reconstruction Loss & PCA Equivalence
content_sections.content:
Autoencoders are optimized by minimizing the difference between the input $\mathbf{x}$ and its reconstruction $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$.

### 1. Reconstruction Loss Functions
*   **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Used when the input features are real-valued continuous variables:

$$J_{\\text{MSE}}(\\theta, \\phi) = \\frac{1}{2} \\sum_{i=1}^N \\lVert \\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \\hat{\\mathbf{x}}^{(i)} \\rVert_2^2$$

*   **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE):** Used when inputs are normalized pixels bounded in $[0, 1]$ (treated as bernoulli probabilities):

$$J_{\\text{BCE}}(\\theta, \\phi) = -\\sum_{i=1}^N \\sum_{j=1}^D \\left( x_j^{(i)} \\log \\hat{x}_j^{(i)} + (1 - x_j^{(i)}) \\log (1 - \\hat{x}_j^{(i)}) \\right)$$

### 2. PCA Equivalence Proof Outline
If an undercomplete autoencoder utilizes strictly linear activation functions (i.e. $\\sigma(z) = z$), the reconstruction is computed as:

$$\\hat{\\mathbf{x}} = \\mathbf{W}_d \\mathbf{W}_e \\mathbf{x}$$

Minimizing the MSE loss over a dataset with zero-mean inputs corresponds to solving:

$$\\min_{\\mathbf{W}_d, \\mathbf{W}_e} \\sum \\lVert \\mathbf{x} - \\mathbf{W}_d \\mathbf{W}_e \\mathbf{x} \\rVert_2^2$$

Mathematically, this optimization problem yields the same projection subspace as **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. The weights $\\mathbf{W}_e$ project the data onto the subspace spanned by the first $d$ principal components of the covariance matrix. Adding non-linear activations (like ReLU or sigmoid) allows the autoencoder to learn non-linear coordinate projections, generalizing PCA to complex non-linear manifolds.
